what_it_does: Spawns two real subprocess workers that share a temporary FsStore to acceptance-test cross-process safety: a lost-update probe on a shared graph, a double-dispatch probe on a shared sequence, and an fsync atomic-write round-trip.
observations: Uses real subprocesses instead of threads to avoid the in-process lock false-pass trap
Each child is wrapped in a hard timeout and killed if it hangs, returning a BLOCKED exit code
Worker logic is embedded as a Python source string and executed via subprocess
Three probes: graph node survival, exactly-once dispatch claim, and fsync'd ref round-trip
Relies on internal Suite/FsStore methods such as _dispatch_lock, dispatch_lock, _already_dispatched, _emit_durable
Uses a global PASS counter incremented only inside the assert helper
Creates isolated temporary store directories and cleans them up after the run
standouts: Real-subprocess concurrency acceptance test with explicit deadlock-safety hard timeout
Worker code is a runtime-generated Python source string executed as a child process
Probes both graph lost-update and exactly-once dispatch claim behavior
conventions: Docstring states the file's purpose, risk model, and test strategy
Uses tempfile for an isolated worktree store
Uses assert with descriptive failure messages
Names acceptance test around a milestone (S4 cross-process safety)
concerns: Depends on undocumented/internal Suite/FsStore methods (_dispatch_lock, dispatch_lock, _already_dispatched, _emit_durable, create_node) that may change
Worker source string is hard to type-check, lint, or statically analyze
Global PASS counter and assert-based control flow make partial failure reporting less structured
Does not directly verify that fcntl is used; only infers cross-process serialization from outcomes
Assumes the nodes directory and NodeRegistry discovery layout match the test environment
notes: A subprocess-based acceptance test that tries to prove two store-sharing processes cannot lose graph updates or double-launch a dispatch, with deadlock-safe timeouts.
questions: Does FsStore.dispatch_lock actually use fcntl, or another cross-process lock?
Are Suite._dispatch_lock, _already_dispatched, and _emit_durable implemented and stable?
What is the exact wire/MCP face architecture referenced in the docstring?
What schema do events_since and load_graph return?
How is the decision.dispatch event persisted and queried by derived_from?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is to prove S4 cross-process safety via fcntl locks and fsync; the actual code exercises behavior through real subprocesses and internal store/suite APIs, validating outcomes rather than directly asserting the locking mechanism.
intention_signals: Trying to provide an end-to-end acceptance guarantee that two faces (bridge and MCP) can safely share one store without lost updates or duplicate dispatches, using real OS-level serialization rather than in-process threading.
novelty: The deliberate rejection of threaded tests as a false-pass trap and the use of real subprocesses with hard-timeout deadlock detection to test cross-process store safety.
contribution: A concrete cross-process acceptance test harness that models the bridge/MCP shared-store boundary and checks no lost-update, no double-dispatch, and durable atomic writes under subprocess concurrency.
summary_for_embedding: A subprocess-based acceptance test for cross-process store safety covering lost-update, exactly-once dispatch, and fsync durability.
intention_for_embedding: To prove that two real processes sharing one FsStore serialize graph mutations and dispatch claims without lost updates or double launches.

symbols.does: Assertion helper that increments a global PASS counter when a condition holds and fails loudly otherwise.
symbols.description: Takes a label and a boolean condition, asserts the condition with a descriptive message, and increments the module-level PASS counter on success; failures abort the test run.

symbols.does: Launches a single worker subprocess that executes the embedded WORKER_SRC against a shared store.
symbols.description: Constructs the environment with CONCURRENCY_ROOT, runs a Python child process with the worker source string, and passes mode, store directory, worker tag, and argument via argv.

symbols.does: Spawns two concurrent workers, hard-timeouts each, and returns parsed JSON results or a BLOCKED reason.
symbols.description: Starts two subprocess workers, waits up to HARD_TIMEOUT seconds, kills any hung children, parses the last JSON line of stdout, and reports deadlock/hang or non-zero exit as a blocked reason.

symbols.does: Sets up isolated temporary stores and runs the three concurrency probes, validating outcomes.
symbols.description: Creates three temporary store directories, runs the graph lost-update probe, the dispatch double-claim probe, and the fsync ref round-trip, then cleans up the worktree.
